summary: Patrick McKenzie reviews Bingo Card Creator's 2008 performance, reporting $20,707 in sales against ~$10,000 in expenses for ~$10,700 profit, exactly hitting his goal of doubling the previous year's revenue. The biggest win was paying a freelancer $1,100 to create niche bingo card content that dramatically improved SEO and organic traffic. Google AdWords Conversion Optimizer continued to be effective despite consuming $5,850. His side project Widget Bakery stalled due to exhausting day job hours, and ChristmasBingoCards.com underperformed expectations. He sets goals of $30,000 in sales, releasing a second product, and potentially going full-time in 2009.
key_points: Niche content creation (paying $1,100 for freelance bingo cards) was the highest-ROI investment, dramatically improving SEO by matching specific search queries to dedicated pages
BCC doubled revenue year-over-year from $10,600 to $20,707 while maintaining roughly the same expense structure, proving the business model scales
The day job's grueling schedule (out at 10 PM plus 2-hour commute) was the primary obstacle to launching a second product that could enable going full-time
Google AdWords consumed 28% of gross revenue but remained worthwhile through Conversion Optimizer, showing the tension between paid acquisition costs and profitability
The business was growing into a small ecosystem supporting five part-time incomes across freelance writers, a designer, and McKenzie himself
money_quotes: The $1,100 paid to a freelancer might stick out in the expenses — I assure you, it is like paying for straw so you can spin it into gold.
Nothing sucks it out of you like getting out of work at 10 PM and then facing a 2 hour commute.
As nice as BCC is I have dreams of eventually going full-time and while BCC provides a quite nice foundation for that I think the next project will be the one that pushes me over the top.
Number of people who sat down at their computer one day and said 'You know, I really need to play some Cheese bingo': 52
SEO is one of my favorite parts of this job — great intellectual challenge, combining a bit of engineering, a lot of marketing, and heaping helpings of pure magic.
And the loom is 95% automatic, whee.
key_insight: A small software business can double revenue by investing modestly in niche content creation that captures long-tail search traffic, even while the founder works a demanding full-time job.
